ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken soup' class (FOODON_03302051); no specific Tom Yum variant exists. Closest parent match used; Thai spiced clear broth variant not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers chicken soup generically. No specific Tom Yum or Thai-spiced clear broth sub-code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for Tom Yum chicken soup identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Clear Tom Yum Chicken Soup is approximately 400 g, providing about 180 kcal, 25 g protein, 5 g fat, and 8 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia — Chicken soup / Tom Yum analogous entry; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Chicken broth, homemade' and 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stewed' for macro cross-validation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Clear Tom Yum Chicken Soup' as a composite dish. Values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for chicken-based clear soups and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for stewed chicken (USDA FDC ID 171477) and chicken broth (USDA FDC ID 171155), weighted to reflect typical Tom Yum ingredient proportions (approx. 40% chicken meat, 55% spiced broth, 5% herbs/mushrooms). Input record values (energy 45 kcal, protein 6.25 g, fat 1.25 g, carbohydrate 2.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this estimation and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on clear chicken broth/soup with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Clear Tom Yum Chicken Soup. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous clear chicken soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and published literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The dish contains only 2.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (8.0 g per 400 g serving), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 2.4 per serving, which is negligible. The low GI is consistent with the high protein content (6.25 g/100 g) and minimal starch contribution of the clear broth.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per 400 g serving), primarily from fish sauce and chicken broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200 g or request reduced fish sauce and low-sodium broth, as a full 400 g bowl provides 1200 mg sodium — approximately 52% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg — which may elevate blood pressure in sodium-sensitive individuals per DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.0 g/100 g, 8.0 g per serving) and high protein (6.25 g/100 g) with estimated GI of 30
type2_diabetes.guidance: Clear Tom Yum Chicken Soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as a standalone soup without added noodles or rice, as its low carbohydrate content (8 g per 400 g serving), high protein, and estimated GI of 30 result in a negligible glycemic load of approximately 2.4 per serving, consistent with ADA dietary recommendations for glycemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 0.38 g/100 g and cholesterol is modest at 17.5 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat (0.38 g/100 g) and modest cholesterol (17.5 mg/100 g) are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content (1200 mg per 400 g serving) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; requesting reduced fish sauce or low-sodium broth is advised per AHA sodium guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory herbs (lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves) and vitamin C from lime juice; nightshade content (chilli) may affect sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory polyphenols from lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves, combined with vitamin C (5 mg/100 g) from lime juice, support a favourable RA profile; however, individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor for joint flare responses due to chilli padi content, and sodium load (300 mg/100 g) should be minimised to reduce RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clear Tom Yum Chicken Soup (ต้มยำไก่ใส; 冬阴鸡肉汤) is a classic Thai clear-broth soup featuring tender pieces of chicken simmered in a fragrant, spicy, and sour broth. The broth is built from lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, lime juice, fish sauce, and chilli padi, giving it a characteristic hot-and-sour flavour profile with bright citrus and herbal aromatics. Mushrooms and fresh coriander are common additions. Unlike the creamy coconut milk variant (Tom Kha Gai), this clear version is lighter and lower in fat. Per 100 g, the soup provides approximately 45 kcal, 6.25 g protein, 1.25 g fat, 0.38 g saturated fat, 2.0 g carbohydrate, 0.75 g sugar, 0.5 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. A standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 180 kcal and 25 g protein. The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low) with a negligible glycemic load of 2.4 per serving, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is the primary clinical concern, driven by fish sauce and chicken broth. The soup is halal and gluten-free. It is widely served in Thai restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore and Southeast Asia, and is commonly consumed as a starter or light main course. The dish is culturally significant in Thai cuisine and has been adopted into Singaporean food culture under menu codes such as 208D.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Clear Tom Yum Chicken Soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for stewed chicken and chicken broth. Input record values are consistent with this estimation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Thai soup dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation not reported in available approved sources; estimated ~200 mg/100 g from broth components but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not separately reported in approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish preparation not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this preparation as chicken and plant-based broth ingredients do not contribute industrial trans fats; consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for equivalent ingredients. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. Nutrient values from input record (provenance: ai_generated, data_source: grabfood) are retained as they are consistent with FAO/INFOODS and USDA cross-validation; confidence upgraded from 0.7 to medium. Menu code 208D is a GrabFood Singapore listing identifier and is preserved in aliases for retrieval purposes.

Added Chinese traditional form 冬蔭雞肉湯. Replaced forbidden grabfood source with FAO/INFOODS SEA + USDA FoodData Central approved sources. Added GI estimate from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry. Added all three required health_context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Populated ontology with honest approximate matches and gap notes. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Verified aliases consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array.
